description: When any RFC-ACDP-0006 §4.1 traversal control (max depth, max total nodes, max fanout per context, total walk timeout) cuts a derived_from walk short and the implementation surfaces a partial result rather than aborting, it MUST distinguish the truncated result from a complete one — e.g. a complete:false flag or an explicit truncation marker in its API result — and MUST NOT present a truncated evidence set as exhaustive. Silent truncation misreads as 'the walk covered everything' exactly where walks are used to assemble the evidence chain behind a conclusion (RFC-ACDP-0006 §2): the unwalked remainder may contain the disputing or superseding context the evaluation needed. Aborting the whole walk with cross_registry_resolution_failed remains conformant (and remains the preferred behavior for fanout overruns); what is non-conformant is a truncated result indistinguishable from a complete one.

input.scenario: Walker resolves a derived_from graph whose root fanout exceeds the configured per-context fanout cap; the implementation is configured to surface partial results instead of aborting

graph_mock.note: Root context's derived_from lists 12 predecessors — 4 more than the mock fanout cap of 8. All 12 exist, are public, and verify; only the cap stops the walk.

additional_test_cases.note: Depth truncation: a linear chain 12 levels deep against max_depth = 10 — the two unwalked tail nodes MUST be reported as an unwalked subtree, per the §4.1 depth row (partial results + cross_registry_resolution_failed indication for the unwalked subtree), never dropped silently.
Timeout truncation: mock registry delays responses so the 30 s end-to-end budget expires mid-walk. Abort-with-error is conformant; a surfaced partial result MUST carry the truncation marker.

expected.behavior: The walk returns the nodes it fetched and verified (8 of 12 predecessors), and the result MUST carry an explicit machine-readable truncation indication — e.g. complete: false, truncated: true, or a typed partial-result marker — distinguishing it from a walk that exhausted the graph. The implementation MUST NOT return the 8-node result in the same shape it uses for a complete walk. The marker SHOULD identify which control fired (max_fanout_per_context) and, where practical, the frontier ctx_ids whose subtrees were not walked.

result_constraints.example_is_illustrative: The exact field names are implementation-defined — this is an API-surface contract, not a wire-format one. What is pinned is the distinction itself: complete and truncated walks MUST be distinguishable by the caller.

expected.also_conformant: Aborting the entire walk with cross_registry_resolution_failed (HTTP 502 if surfaced via a registry endpoint; consumer-side: an equivalent typed error) instead of surfacing a partial result. For fanout overruns this fail-closed behavior remains RECOMMENDED per the §4.1 control table.
expected.non_conformant_implementation: A walker that, on hitting any traversal cap, returns the nodes gathered so far in the same result shape as a completed walk — no flag, no marker, no error — so the caller cannot tell a bounded walk from an exhausted graph.
expected.rationale: The traversal caps are resource-bounding controls, not evidence filters. A consumer assembling the evidence set behind a decision (RFC-ACDP-0006 §2) must be able to tell 'these are all the predecessors' from 'these are the predecessors I had budget for'; conflating the two silently converts a denial-of-service defense into an integrity failure.
